nativeRange.habitat: Sinularia species inhabit tropical reef environments across a wide depth range, from shallow, brightly lit reef crests down to deeper and more turbid reef slopes, and have been recorded at depths from 1 to 37 metres.

decorPreferences: position away from SPS and sensitive LPS corals
downstream from water flow to carry shed mucus away

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae; does not require target feeding to thrive. Some hobbyists report benefit from occasional broadcast feeding of fine phytoplankton or zooplankton, though this is considered supplemental rather than necessary.

goodWith: other soft corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: Acropora and other SPS corals within 15-20 cm
sensitive LPS corals in close proximity
corals positioned directly downstream of Sinularia without carbon filtration

imageCandidates.notes: DoroshenkoE, 2642x1800. Vivid neon-green fluorescent leather coral (S. dura, cabbage form) under blue reef lighting in a display aquarium - sharp, whole colony, the exact fluorescent hobbyist look; strongest actinic in-tank shot.
Neville Wootton, 2892x2304. Vivid salmon-pink lettuce/cabbage leather (S. brassica) in-situ against a pink reef wall - sharp, striking colour, whole colony.
Nick Hobgood, 1500x2000. Cream spaghetti-finger leather (S. flexibilis) in-situ - sharp, clearly shows the long trailing finger polyps diagnostic of the genus.
Haplochromis, 2164x2040. Wild brown lobed cabbage-leather (S. dura) in-situ - sharp, shows the flat lobed morphology of the Cabbage Leather trade form in natural colour.
George Berninger Jr., 2048x1536. Tan branching finger leather (S. flexibilis) established in a reef aquarium - shows the typical captive display appearance.
Albert kok, 6000x4000. Reef scene with cream finger-leather colonies (S. flexibilis) and shoaling anthias - sharp, high-res, shows the coral in natural reef context.
Diego Delso, 5337x3558. Pale-green knobby lobed colony (S. densa, now placed in Sclerophytum, close ally within Alcyoniidae) in-situ - very sharp, whole colony. Caveat: recent taxonomic split.
Erez Shoham (van Ofwegen et al. 2016), 1512x979. Gold/tan knobby-lobed colonies (S. levi) in-situ - sharp, shows the digitate knobby lobe form.
Erez Shoham (van Ofwegen et al. 2016), 1512x1041. Grey-blue variant of S. levi in-situ - sharp, adds colour variety (blue-grey morph) versus the gold form.
Philippe Bourjon, 1200x862. Grey encrusting knobby colony (Sinularia sp.) on sand in-situ - sharp, whole colony; muted colour so filler.
Philippe Bourjon, 1200x967. Whitish lobed leather colony in-situ - sharp, whole colony. Caveat: uploader lists ID (S. schlagmani) as unconfirmed.

summary: Sinularia Coral is a genus of leather soft corals encompassing more than 160 described species and is widely regarded as one of the more beginner-friendly choices in reef-keeping. All species are photosynthetic and are generally advised to be kept well clear of SPS corals, as they release chemicals that can inhibit neighboring coral growth and periodically shed a waxy outer film.
careNotes:
Sinularia is a large genus of over 160 described species, all sharing near-identical husbandry requirements. Sinularia corals are generally regarded as one of the more forgiving corals in the reef hobby, tolerating moderate swings in water quality that would stress SPS corals. They are photosynthetic and have been reported to thrive without any supplemental feeding, though occasional broadcast feeding of phytoplankton or fine zooplankton tends to support growth and polyp extension.

Key care considerations:

• Lighting: Moderate lighting is generally advised — sources suggest approximately 75-200 PAR works well for most specimens. The coral has been documented adapting to a range of intensities, including lower-light areas that many other corals cannot tolerate. Gradual acclimation when changing lighting is recommended to prevent bleaching.

• Flow: Moderate indirect flow is preferred. Strong, broad water movement around the colony has been known to assist the natural shedding process and prevent detritus settling on the tissue. Direct high-intensity jets tend to inhibit polyp extension.

• Allelopathy (chemical warfare): Sinularia corals release terpenoid compounds — including the sesquiterpene sinulariolide and related metabolites — that have been documented inhibiting the growth of neighboring corals, particularly SPS species such as Acropora. This allelopathic influence is generally considered to extend approximately 15-20 cm from the colony edge. Toxin output has been reported to intensify during the shedding cycle, when stressed, or when competing for space. Running activated carbon in the sump is commonly recommended to adsorb released terpenoids. Regular partial water changes also help dilute accumulated compounds.

• Periodic shedding: One of the most distinctive Sinularia behaviors is the periodic shedding of the outer waxy cuticle. The coral will retract its polyps, develop a dull or filmy appearance, and eventually slough off a mucus-like waxy film — a process that tends to last roughly 1-2 weeks. This is a healthy, normal cleaning behavior that removes debris, algae, and fouling organisms from the colony surface. Good indirect water flow has been reported to facilitate more efficient shedding. The shed material and associated chemical release can temporarily elevate toxin levels in the tank; increased carbon filtration during shedding episodes is commonly advised.

• Placement: Sinularia has been noted to be broadly adaptable to different positions in the tank (lower, middle, or upper rockwork depending on lighting intensity). The primary placement constraint is allelopathic: the colony should be positioned with at least 15-20 cm clear space from SPS corals and other chemically sensitive species.

• Growth rate: The genus tends to exhibit a moderate to fast growth rate in healthy conditions. Propagation by fragging is straightforward — frags cut from branch tips or the colony lobes are known to attach readily to plugs or rubble.

• Supplements: Some sources recommend dosing iodine, strontium, and trace elements; however, these are supplemental and not strictly required in a reef system with regular water changes.

• Cabbage Leather variant (S. dura): The 'Cabbage Leather' trade form (principally Sinularia dura) has a lobed, flat morphology rather than branching fingers and has been described as particularly toxic among the leather coral genera. Care requirements are otherwise similar to the finger-leather forms.
breedingNotes: Fragging is the primary propagation method in captivity and is widely practiced. Branches can be cut with scissors or bone cutters; frags readily attach to plugs, rubble, or rock substrate and encrust quickly. Sexual reproduction (broadcast spawning) has been documented in wild populations but is not a practical captive breeding method.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Sinularia Finger Leather Coral
primary.notes: Used for water parameters (temperature 72-78°F, SG 1.023-1.025, pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12), care level (Easy), placement (bottom to middle), feeding, and native range (Kenya, Tonga, Melanesia, Australia). Confirmed semi-aggressive temperament and toxin-production note.

additional.name: Extreme Corals – Sinularia Care Requirements
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 76-80°F (24.4-26.7°C), salinity 1.024-1.026, pH 8.1-8.4, alkalinity 8-10 dKH, calcium 400-450 ppm, magnesium 1250-1350 ppm. PAR range stated as 100-250. Minor disagreement on temperature lower bound resolved by averaging across sources. Placement middle to upper.

additional.name: BiologyInsights – Sinularia Coral Care
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature 75-80°F (24-27°C), salinity 1.025-1.026, pH 8.1-8.4, alkalinity 8-12 dKH, calcium 380-420 ppm, magnesium 1200-1350 ppm. PAR 50-150. Confirmed Indo-Pacific native range (Fiji, Tonga, Solomon Islands, Great Barrier Reef) and shedding behavior (waxy film lasting ~2 weeks). Provided taxonomy: Family Alcyoniidae.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens – Green Sinularia Leather
additional.notes: Confirmed moderate lighting (PAR 50-150 cited), beginner care level, shedding behavior (~2-week waxy film), and Indo-Pacific origin (Fiji, Tonga, Solomon Islands, GBR). Described toxin release as 'variably toxic' within genus.

additional.name: SaltwaterAquariumBlog – Cabbage Leather Coral (S. dura)
additional.notes: Species-specific page for Sinularia dura (Cabbage Leather form). Temperature 73-84°F (22.8-28.9°C), SG 1.025, pH 8.1-8.4, kH 8-12. Described as 'most toxic of the leather coral species' releasing terpenes that inhibit stony coral growth. Native range Indian and Pacific Oceans and Red Sea.
